warnings: logP from semi-empirical ΔG_solv differences is screening-grade; ±1 log unit typical.

Each leg is an electronic-energy difference on a single frozen geometry — no ZPE/thermal/entropy and no per-phase relaxation. The reported delta_G_solv_* legs are electronic only; the 1 atm→1 M standard-state term cancels in the water–octanol difference, so logP itself is insensitive to it, but the individual legs are not corrected ΔG*_solv.
For chemoinformatic logP, also consider RDKit's Crippen/XLogP (group-contribution).

Single-conformer result: every species was evaluated at ONE geometry, with no conformational/Boltzmann averaging. For a flexible molecule the Boltzmann-averaged free energy over conformers can differ from a single conformer by several kcal/mol — shifting pKa by >1 unit, E° by >0.1 V, and logP by >0.5. If the molecule has rotatable bonds, run conformer-search first and average, or treat this as a screening estimate tied to the input geometry.

prose:
Method provenance block:
Property: octanol–water partition coefficient (log P, dimensionless)
Level of theory: GFN2-xTB (semi-empirical tight-binding) as implemented in xtb (backend version not reported)
Geometry provenance: supplied by user (toluene.xyz, 15 atoms); single-point energies on this fixed geometry (no optimization)
Solvent/environment: implicit ALPB solvation models for water and for octanol; the gas-phase geometry was reused for both solvated single points (no per-phase relaxation). Charge 0, multiplicity 1.
Software: xtb via chemkit logp skill; command recorded in result JSON (see cli_invocation)
Convergence/caveats: not an optimization; single-point energies converged. The engine returned the warnings reproduced above; no additional issues flagged.

Headline result (GFN2-xTB, single-conformer): log P ≈ 2.3344329 (dimensionless).

Reproducibility: calculation was run through chemkit’s logp skill; the full command is in cli_invocation and the live JSON at /var/folders/ym/_02kbfyd29z17l376783nlm00000gn/T/tmprvp_ybv4/live.json captures every field returned by the engine.
